title: Dad and daughter defy doubters on epic voyage from Newcastle to Norway
text:
There is following in your father's footsteps, but what about sailing in his wake?

Rob Donald, 59, and his daughter Freya are sailing an 89-year-old, 9.8-metre wooden yacht from Newcastle to Norway.

Mr Donald is an avid sailor and has captained boats for more than 30 years, but said making this epic voyage with his 19-year-old daughter presented a whole new challenge.

"People said we wouldn't last a week together,” he said.

Return to roots

The pair set sail from Lake Macquarie in March, 2025 and have since logged 18,000 nautical miles (30,000 kilometres).

Rob Donald bought Misha 37 years ago in Amsterdam, Netherlands. (Supplied: Rob Donald)

Mr Donald's wife Hanne is from Norway and the family lived there for several years.

But it was in France in 1988 where he found the other love of his life, a small wooden yacht he later named Misha.

He sailed the yacht back to Australia, and has since taken it on a few voyages to the Great Barrier Reef.

Rob Donald completing some running repairs on Misha in 1993. (Supplied: Rob Donald)

But it was always Mr Donald's goal to sail Misha back to the Netherlands, where she was built, and then on to Norway.

"To get back to where Misha was built, that's a real milestone, so the [sailing yard] is excited," he said.

Mr Donald said it took months of planning to make the epic adventure a reality, modernising the boat to withstand rough seas, installing an autopilot and building a new mast complete with new sails.

However, his original plan to have his wife to join him on the trip proved the first snag.

"[She said]: 'You're dreaming,'" he laughed.

But my daughter said: 'Dad, I'll go with you.'"

The pair set sail from Lake Macquarie, NSW in March 2025. (Supplied: Rob Donald)

For Freya it was an easy decision, even if the sleeping arrangement on the small vessel leaves a little to be desired.

"I have always loved the boat, I wanted to prove him wrong that I could last more than a week," she said.

"I sleep on the double bunk when we are anchored but when we are at sea, sometimes it leaks."

Expect the unexpected

They say calm seas rarely make a skilled sailor, and it was when the father-daughter duo hit the open ocean that their biggest hurdle emerged.

Doctors told Mr Donald he had high levels of prostate-specific antigen before he left, which prompted him to get re-tested in Cape Town.

"I found out I had pretty aggressive prostate cancer; I got infected and ended up in intensive care," he said.

"It wasn't a great experience."

Rob Donald says he was one of the first people in NSW to have a single port robotic to remove his prostate. (Supplied: Rob Donald)

Mr Donald flew back to Australia in December last year for surgery, and three weeks later was back in Cape Town.

"I'm fit as a fiddle [now]," he said.

From Cape Town, they sailed to Namibia and eventually to Ascension Island in the South Atlantic Ocean, an isolated volcanic island notorious for sharks.

"We had a rudder problem, so I had to get in the water to go under the vessel to check the rudder," Mr Donald said

Freya enjoyed her 19th birthday at sea. (Supplied: Rob Donald)

"I didn't see any sharks, but when we left we caught a yellow fin tuna and a massive tiger shark came and took half."

Freya said one of her cherished memories from the trip was their visit to Madasgascar.

"I was with the lemurs and being on that quiet island with lovely people … that was very fun and beautiful."

The final voyage

Rob Donald says he is likely to bid bon voyage to Misha at the end of this journey. (Supplied: Rob Donald)

With their epic 15-month journey drawing to a close, the Donalds are beginning to prepare for what is next.



Mr Donald said he intended to stay in Norway for a while, while Freya looks to begin her own career.

"I want to get a job on a boat and hopefully become a captain one day," she said.

As for the future of Misha, a tough decision still needs to be made.

"I've offered the boat to my kids, to see if they want to continue the legacy," Mr Donald said.

"It's still in great condition, but I'm sort of ready to walk inland with my anchor until someone stops me to say, what is that."

title: Love story: How grieving at the dog park led to a second chance at love
text:
Jorgen and Jill Porse, both aged in their 70s, were in the depths of grief when they first met at their local dog park 15 years ago. It was their playful pooches that introduced them, but their shared understanding of loss that sparked a connection.

Whether it's romantic, platonic, heartbreaking or surprising, our Love Story series is about love in all its forms. Share yours with us.

Jill: My husband had passed away. Before that, he said it would be good for me to get a little dog so I wouldn't be so lonely.

I used to fill in my afternoons in my grief taking Coco, my labradoodle, to the dog park.

I used to see Jorgen standing there. He was dressed in light white shorts and a top, and I used to think he must be a ferry worker or a painter.

I later found out he was a baker.

Jill and Jorgen Porse's dogs Joey and Coco were the reason the couple met. (Supplied)

Jorgen: I'd lost my son in an accident about the same time Jilly lost her husband.

We [my then wife and I] got the dog after, I suppose as something meaningful to do and deal with the realities.

I took Joey, he was a Siberian husky, to the same park as Jilly.

The two dogs got along really well and we would walk and talk.

Life moves on

Jorgen: I was in a broken marriage after the death of my son.

It's something people obviously find extremely difficult to deal with.

There is such a high divorce rate. I was determined not to fall into that, but it still happened.

But life moves on and there is always something on the horizon.

Sign up to the ABC Lifestyle newsletter Get a mid-week boost and receive easy recipes, wellbeing ideas, and home and garden tips in your inbox every Wednesday. You’ll also receive a monthly newsletter of our best recipes.

Jill: With my husband passing away, all my dreams in life felt shattered.

He had cancer but it was a quick exit. I was in so much shock, and you think, well, what is left?

At first, I wasn't interested in meeting anyone else.

But a year after meeting Jorgen, he invited me out for his birthday and it kind of went from there.

We really connected. We were very compatible. We both had a big interest in travel.

I had two daughters and grandchildren, but meeting Jorgen opened up another life with someone else.

My daughters were pleased I had met someone.

Jorgen: My other son was extremely happy on our behalf. He loves Jilly and the rest of the family equal to his own.

On our way to Las Vegas for a holiday, I thought this could be a bit of an interesting twist to the whole thing, and decided to propose on the plane.

I got all the staff involved. I popped the question on the top deck of the A380.

Jill: It was very romantic. It took me by total surprise. Anyway, I said yes!

Jorgen: I few years later we went back to Vegas and got married there. It was a fantastic wedding on the rooftop of a hotel, overlooking the strip.

Jorgen proposed to Jill in the air while travelling to Las Vegas, where they later returned to be married. (Supplied)

A new chapter, together

Jill: You can love more than once, you can never replace, but you can have a totally new story, a new chapter.

I'm grateful for this new chapter with someone.

Coco and Joey are no longer with us, but we now have a whippet called Rufus.

That's our focus, on this beautiful dog, and we still go to the same dog park and meet with the same people.

title: Quick hits: Salah's class towards Aussies, Paraguay labelled a 'disgrace'
text:
Mo Salah's classy post-penalty act to the stricken Socceroos and a Paraguay team blasted as a "disgrace".

Also, Jesse Marsch goes all Bazball after Canada's defeat, as a goalkeeper goes walkabout.

Here are today's World Cup quick hits.

1. Paraguay acts out as France plays up

France came into their clash as heavy favourites, and absolutely dominated a Paraguay team who have at best stumbled, at worst kicked, their way into the last 16 of the tournament.

France made 568 passes and had 76 per cent possession, while Paraguay only made 183. France made more successful passes than that in the final third alone (199).

But it was still a desperate slog for Les Bleus, who only scored thanks to a VAR awarded penalty for a foul on Désiré Doué.

Players came together after Kylian Mbappé was hauled down. (Getty Images: Anadolu/Abdulhamid Hosbas)

The frustration was clearly rising for the French, with Paraguay unashamedly deploying more dark arts than you'd read about in a Harry Potter book.

The Paraguayan team did everything possible to disrupt the penalty being taken, with Gustavo Velázquez sneaking in behind the referee to dig up the penalty spot with two, well-aimed prods with his boot.

It didn't matter, Kylian Mbappé scored and ran off with a mocking grin on his face.

He smiled even through all the niggle throughout the rest of the game, with Mbappé being kicked out at by Juan José Cáceres. There was an elbow into the chest of Dayot Upamecano by Gabriel Ávalos. A collection of embarrassing dives from any number of players, and the French largely kept their cool.

Gustavo Velázquez dug up the penalty spot when the referee was not looking. (Supplied: SBS)

"The Paraguay players were an absolute disgrace tonight. If they were on my team, I'd be dragging half of them off the pitch," former English goalkeeper Joe Hart told the BBC.

"I would never want to win that way and I would never want to play football that way.

"The fact that not one Paraguayan was booked inside the 90 minutes is astonishing."

They gave away more fouls than the French, who were shown three by referee Ilgiz Tantashev of Uzbekistan.

"That is probably the worse referee performance I've seen at this tournament," former Germany midfielder Thomas Hitzlsperger told the BBC, potentially with vested interests given the Paraguayan's knocked Germany out in the round of 32.

But then again, who knows how Paraguay would have gone at this World Cup if they'd tried to play football?

2. A stinker in Philadelphia

As the heat rose on the pitch thanks to Paraguay's deployment of the dark arts, the temperature was well and truly high off the pitch as well.

The US National Weather Service said the city hit a high of 38.3C.

Players needed to do everything possible to say cool in the Philadelphia heat. (Getty Images: FIFA/Patrick Smith)

That is far from the hottest ever World Cup game played, though.

When the US last hosted the World Cup, in in 1994, Mexico played Ireland in Orlando when the temperature reached a staggering 43C.

A midday kick off and a Florida summer was never going to be a good idea, but Ireland still managed to claim a 1-0 victory.

3. Moral wins for Marsch?

Losing to Morocco left Canada coach Jesse Marsch sick to the stomach.

Having watched his side come flying out of the blocks and absolutely dominating the early exchanges, by the time of the first hydration break Canada had managed four shots to Morocco's nil, and had 12 touches in the opposition's box, to Morocco's nil.

That being said, Morocco turned it up after half time and won 3-0.

And although Marsch had every reason to be incredibly proud of his side, the former Leeds United manager did go a bit Bazball in his post-match comments.

Jesse Marsch said he would rather be in his team's shoes than Morocco's. (Getty Images: FIFA/Hector Vivas)

"We were the better team. They made a couple more plays than us but cracking up the intensity was not the issue, it's just they had a bit of quality in the final third and we lacked the ability to make a play when we needed to," Marsch said.

"In terms of match the plan, [the] idea, believing in themselves and taking a top team to the limit, we were the much better team in the first half and even the beginning of the second half.

"What a privilege our fans have to follow a team like this that goes after the game like this and doesn't play defensive and shows they can be better.

"I'd rather be us than them. As good as Morocco are, I'd rather be us. I couldn't be happier."

I bet Morocco will be thinking exactly that as they line up for their quarterfinal.

4. Rush keeper

There is nothing better than seeing a team throw caution to the wind, whatever the sport is.

In football, the best illustration of this is when a goalkeeper abandons his goal and advances to the ratified air of the opposition penalty box, just as Maxime Crépeau did in Houston. Twice.

This is very much a break-glass in case of an emergency option, not unlike the benching of a goal tender in ice hockey when teams are searching for a goal to get them back into the contest, and was absolutely necessary for Canada, trailing as they did 2-0.

Maxime Crépeau couldn't score past Yassine Bounou. (Getty Images: Lars Baron)

And there is precedent for it to work too.

In the 98th minute of a Champions League tie against Real Madrid in January this year, in fact, Benfica's Ukranian keeper Anatoliy Trubin scored a last-gasp header to give Benfica a 4-2 win, as well as improving the team's goal difference enough to qualify for the play-offs.

Brazilian keeper Alisson Bekker scored in a similar way for Liverpool in 2021, glancing home from Trent Alexander-Arnold's corner to become just the sixth keeper to score a goal in Premier League history, and the first to score a winner.

The 32-year-old Crépeau twice came forward as Canada pushed for a last-gasp moment of quality or chaos in the death throes of its World Cup campaign.

Crépeau was always up against scoring though. No goalkeeper has ever scored in the opposition goal in World Cup history.

And such was the case here too, when on neither occasion could he make contact, with Yassine Bounou punching clear.

Bounou's intervention was a blow for Canada in more ways than one — the Moroccan custodian was actually born in Montreal to Moroccan parents, moving back to north Africa aged three.

5. Salah's class in victory

There may be no worse feeling in sport than being knocked out of a tournament via a penalty shoot out.

Conversely, there can be no greater release of joy than prevailing in one, the juxtaposing feelings playing out at opposite ends of the field.

Mo Salah went straight over to the beaten Australian players. (Getty Images: FIFA/Ryan Pierse)

So as almost all Egyptian players tore off after Hossam Abdelmaguid following his successful fourth penalty, and Australia's brave Socceroos stood motionless on half way, it was heartening to see Egypt's totemic captain Mo Salah walk over to the vanquished Socceroos to shake their hands.

Only after had he congratulated his opponents, did he go and celebrate.

The ferocity of elite sport often relegates such sporting moments to glimpses of light amidst a maelstrom of aggression.

Which make moments like Salah's shine all the brighter, and is why they need to be celebrated.

title: Banker-turned-barefoot artist and ceramicist recreate Klimt's The Kiss
text:
The Kiss by Austrian painter Gustav Klimt has captured the imagination of generations of art lovers as one of the world's most recognisable artworks.

Now, a Queensland-based couple has recreated the 1908 piece in painstaking detail through a glass and ceramic mural, which took six years to complete.

It's been a labour of love, which has brought the couple, muralist Brett Campbell and ceramicist Kari Didi, closer, and renewed their love of art.

"At the time, we … said, 'Oh, this could take a whole year.' That was six years ago," Didi said.

The mosaic of Klimt's iconic painting is displayed at the Maleny Yoga Shed. (Supplied: Sunshine Coast Videography)

Banker to barefoot artist

Campbell was a bank manager struggling with corporate life when he discovered mosaics.

"Some days, I would actually be crying on my way to work. I hated it so much. I hated it," he said.

" I hated the way we treated people and … I hated having so many bosses. "

Campbell's weekends of pottering in the garden barefoot and making mosaics were the inspiration to leave his 9-5.

"One day, he walked out of the bank saying, 'I cannot do this anymore. It's soul destroying,'" Didi said.

" He threw away his shoes and his tie and said, 'I will never wear either again.' And he hasn't. "

Barefoot ceramics an OHS 'nightmare'

Going barefoot while cutting glass for elaborate mosaics sounds like it could go wrong very quickly.

Campbell is barefoot full time and said he'd become very conscious of where he was stepping.

"Part of the reason I went barefoot initially was to harden my feet up so I could work on ceramics," he said.

"And now I can walk on glass or anything and it doesn't really seem to penetrate."

But he's still careful where he walks.

Kari Didi and Brett Campbell working on the "massive jigsaw". (Supplied: Sunshine Coast Videography)

The Kissing Project

When Campbell first suggested recreating the world's most famous kiss through mosaic, he wrote it off as too challenging because of the amount of gold involved.

Didi's ceramicist skills came to the fore, using gold glaze and creating specialty tiles for the artwork.

Despite her creativity in utilising alternative techniques, the finished piece required a lot of gold leaf, which has exploded in price, more than doubling over the past six years.

Six years ago booklets of gold leaf cost $95 a piece, now it's about $230.

"That's how much gold has risen in these past few years," Didi said. "So, there's thousands of dollars of actual gold in it, millions of hours of work in it."

As the mosaic grew, so did the relationship.

"Over six years, of course, our relationship has developed," Didi said.

" We'd both look forward to those days when we'd get to do this massive jigsaw together. "

The Kiss mosaic is finished with thousands of dollars of gold leaf. (Supplied: Sunshine Coast Videography)

What's next?

The life-sized mosaic, measuring 180cm x 180cm, is on display at the Maleny Yoga Shed.

It's a weighty piece, which requires a strong wall to hold it, but the couple hopes to lend it to a space where it can be admired by the community before finding its forever home.

Campbell and Did plan to use the funds from the sale of the piece to start a community art studio and, of course, another collaboration.

"It wouldn't surprise me if we decide to make another artwork. We might get bored without one together, I reckon," Campbell said.

title: International experts weigh in on Australia’s bird flu response
text:
Australia's best bet at managing the spread of the deadly H5 strain of bird flu will come from focusing heavily on testing and surveillance, according to two globally recognised infectious disease experts.

On Saturday, the virus was officially detected further north than previously found, when an infected giant petrel discovered in New South Wales was confirmed by the CSIRO to have the H5N1 strain — becoming the sixth confirmed case in Australia.

Scientists are also testing to confirm if another migratory bird found on a beach in Perth's north has the virus, after returning an initial positive test.

Nahid Bhadelia says not conducting enough testing was a mistake in the US. (ABC News: Jake Sturmer)

In addition to the NSW case, five birds have been found with H5N1 on the southern coasts of WA and South Australia.

Nahid Bhadelia has been monitoring the spread of the disease across the world as it resulted in more than 633 million animals dying or being culled.

The director of Boston University's Biothreats Emergence, Analysis and Communications Network urged Australia to learn from the mistakes of her country, the main one of which was not testing enough.

Loading...

"What we failed to do is really understand for a long period of time how it was transmitting on farms," she said.

" And I think this is where Australia … with its biosecurity, with additional testing, could get ahead of and really understand how in your particular [environment] it's going to play out. "

Testing of local birds, marine mammals a priority

The immediate priority, Dr Bhadelia said, should be additional surveillance of resident birds and marine mammals, which would likely be infected before the virus spread to farm animals like chickens.

World Organisation for Animal Health head Emmanuelle Soubeyran said that was one of the key lessons to learn from the virus's spread around the rest of the world.

The virus has spread throughout the world, including Brazil where this dead porpoise was found. (Reuters: Diego Vara)

"Surveillance is very important, rapid laboratory diagnostic and genomic sequencing to really understand where … does the virus come from," she told the ABC from Paris.

With a background in veterinary public health, she commended Australia for quickly detecting the disease, but said the next step was to balance passive surveillance — testing sick or dead birds which are found by members of the public — with active surveillance.

Loading...

"But then you need to know where to do this active surveillance, which means that you need a risk-based surveillance in poultry, in backyards, in wild birds also," Dr Soubeyran said.

"It is a combination of these, both passive and active surveillance, that is important."

NSW and WA ramp up surveillance

Lindall Kidd, the national migratory shorebird coordinator for BirdLife Australia, said there was a definite need for increased active surveillance, given WA in particular had largely relied on public reporting so far.

"We really need to focus on sort of actively walking the beaches, looking for potentially infected … seabirds," she said.

"It's really positive that some of the other jurisdictions have actually rapidly mobilised to do this active surveillance, because that can actually help prevent a possible outbreak of this virus."

The brown skua is one of the migratory birds that has been found in Australia with bird flu. (iNaturalist: Oscar Thomas, Brown skua, CC BY-NC 4.0)

On Friday, WA Agriculture Minister Jackie Jarvis said the state's fisheries officers had been monitoring key locations across remote islands on WA's southern coast for ill or dead animals.

She said that would extend to Perth beaches following the latest suspected case, as well as wetlands where waterbirds were known to gather.

Jackie Jarvis says testing is being expanded to Perth beaches. (ABC News: Jake Sturmer)

Her New South Wales counterpart said that state had about 500 people, including government staff and private vets, working on surveillance.

"They're checking this area of the state around Hawks Nest and the mid north coast, and other high-risk parts of the state, looking for birds," Agriculture Minister Tara Moriarty said.

The giant petrel is another type of bird detected in Australia with H5N1. (Supplied: Lori-Ann Shibish/Esperance Wildlife Hospital and Sanctuary)

"That includes fisheries officers over the water, that includes people in national parks, that includes people in agriculture, looking for birds that might look ill so that we can see if there are any more.

"That surveillance has been increased now and will continue over the weekend."

Investing in an 'army'

Both international experts said the information provided by surveillance would be critical in deciding what steps would be effective to control the strain's spread.

What to do if you see sick or dead birds? Do not touch sick or dead birds or animals.

Record details and take photos or video.

Report the animal to the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline on 1800 675 888.

"Unfortunately one of the major ways that we control it around the world is if you find it in a particular cluster of birds … you have to cull them, particularly for commercial poultry," Dr Bhadelia said.

"Or if you quickly detect it in migratory birds or a population of migratory birds, it's to isolate that area and to do surveillance in it."

That relied on having strong veterinary services, testing capability and strong relationships between different groups that interact with wildlife.

If H5N1 makes it into poultry populations, culls will likely have to occur. (ABC News: Morgan Timms)

Perhaps the biggest mistake other countries had made, Dr Soubeyran said, was not to fund those efforts.

"It's important to invest when it's peacetime, to have an army that is able to respond when it is war, when you have the crisis," she said.

"We need all policymakers and decision-makers of the world [to] understand that animal health is not a cost, but it is really an investment."

Biosecurity awareness

Dr Bhadelia and Dr Soubeyran agreed those measures meant it was not inevitable the disease would spread through agricultural populations if the right control measures were implemented.

The numbers showing H5's deadly rise The deadly H5 bird flu has finally hit Australia. Here's how other nations have coped with the virus.

That could also limit the impact of the virus on consumers, by keeping supply high enough that prices would not significantly increase.

"The most important is to, and that's what Australia is doing, is really to raise awareness of the importance of biosecurity at the farm level in order to … avoid contamination of [farms]," Dr Soubeyran said.

Dr Bhadelia added that the risk of the version of the disease in Australia spreading to cattle was relatively low, because it was different to the strain that had impacted the industry in the United States.

title: Farmers stuck with 'broken' standalone power systems
text:
Farmers in rural Western Australia say they have had to grapple with power outages and frequent maintenance issues after being moved off the grid and on to standalone power systems by Western Power.

A standalone power system which typically consists of solar panels, a battery and a back-up diesel generator was installed on Ben Parsons's farm in Tenterden, about 340 kilometres south of Perth, in 2022.

"We probably used to get three or four power outages a year and we thought it's going to be great, we'll have power all year round," Mr Parsons said.

" Since it's been installed, we've had untold power outages, probably getting up towards 70 power outages. "

A standalone system has solar panels, a generator and batteries supplied by Western Power. (ABC Great Southern: Mark Bennett)

He has asked Western Power to switch him back on to the main grid, but the utility rejected his request.

"We've had kids in the bath, and the power's dropped out, 7 o'clock at night middle of winter pitch black, you've got kids screaming in the bathroom," he said.

"It sounds like the generator is right outside the kids' bedroom. My seven-year-old said to me the other day, 'Oh Dad what's that noise I can't get to sleep' … and it was the generator running."

Western Power had pitched the systems as a greener and more reliable alternative to the grid that would also help cut costs by reducing the utility's reliance on aging poles and wires.

No community consultation

A total of 498 standalone units have been installed in regional WA, with 100 in the Shire of Cranbrook alone.

The shire's chief executive Linda Gray said there had been a lack of community consultation.

"We had a phone call from an 85-year-old lady located at the northern end of the shire and she had had people arrive on her property to tell her that her power was being cut off and she was going to have a standalone power system," she said.

" She had no community engagement, no awareness of what this was about until these people turned up on her doorstep. "

Linda Gray is the CEO of the Cranbrook shire that has approximately 100 standalone systems on farms and properties. (ABC Great Southern: Mark Bennett)

Residents on the standalone power systems still pay per unit for electricity, the same as consumers on the main grid.

Ms Gray said that under WA legislation Western Power was not required to get consent from the landowner for the change.

"They told me that if you lock your gates so they can't go onto the property that they can get a warrant," she said.

Initial pilot

Many residents who received standalone systems during the initial roll-out say they have been nothing but problematic, with Western Power taking years to fix them.

Owen Grahame, who lives in South Newdegate, got a standalone system installed 10 years ago and started experiencing problems about five years ago.

Owen Grahame stands under his Western Power standalone power solar array on his farm at South Newdegate. (ABC Great Southern: Mark Bennett)

He has had to run the generator almost constantly due to ongoing problems with the system, including broken solar panels.

Western Power only recently contacted him about replacing the system.

"It starts up even in full sun every few days; something is clearly not right with it," Mr Grahame said.

Former WA energy minister Mike Nahan launched the trial of the standalone power systems in 2016. (ABC Great Southern: Mark Bennett)

For farmers like Bernie Giles in Northern Ravensthorpe, the system has had issues from the start.

"It's always had the same problem ever since its original installation and that is it produces its own heat and it doesn't get rid of it," he said.

"There was a circuit there for the air conditioner, but it was actually taken out because they were trying to raise the efficiency because it was one of the first models that were put out."

Bernie Giles's family at North Ravensthorpe was one of the first families to be switched off from the grid 10 years ago. (ABC Great Southern: Mark Bennett)

Mr Giles has also now been promised an upgraded system.

A Western Power spokesperson said the utility was working closely with customers to ensure these systems were fit for purpose.

"We recognise that some initial units required remediation and upgrades as we've learnt more about individual customer needs," the spokesperson said.

"The standalone power program is delivering strong outcomes for customers, with 96 per cent of users experiencing better reliability since installation."

title: France finally breaks free of stubborn Paraguay on Independence Day
text:
This round of 16 clash at Philadelphia Stadium opened with all the glamour, excitement and showmanship that you would expect from a game featuring the red-hot tournament favourite. Except that was all before kick-off.

On the USA's national holiday, the Fourth of July, in the city called the birthplace of America, the pre-game show was full of all the red, white and blue patriotism your heart could desire.

A banging rendition of the Star-Spangled Banner from Broadway star Idina Menzel, a carefully choreographed display of stars and flags covering the pitch, a live band, and fighter jets flying overhead.

There were more than a few founding fathers spotted around the stadium too in their full regalia, an impressive feat considering the sweltering conditions with the temperature pushing 40 degrees.

And that was as good as it got for the next 70 minutes.

Until Kylian Mbappé's penalty finally broke the stalemate against Paraguay and sent the battling Les Bleus through to the quarterfinals.

Kylian Mbappé celebrates scoring a penalty, the only goal of the match. (Getty Images: FIFA/Patrick Smith)

Heated exchanges

The French had been an attacking delight at this World Cup, scoring at least three goals in all four of its previous games.

But ever since a stinging opening 4-1 defeat to the USA, Paraguay has made its own impact as the underdog nation building its surprising run on stoic defence, fouls — some awarded, many not, forcing turnovers, and frustrating the opposition.

The physicality attracted criticism from many fans and pundits during the game, including former England defender Stephen Warnock speaking on the BBC.

"There is playing fair and tough, but this is playing tough and unfair," he said.

"Paraguay are trying to injure players and that is unacceptable."

Tensions boiled over several times during the game. (Getty Images: Anadolu/Abdulhamid Hosbas)

Interestingly, the only yellow cards were handed to three French players.

Les Bleus did not even come close to scoring in the first half, failing to register a shot on target as Paraguay camped everyone behind the ball.

France's players grew angry with the stray kicks directed at them, Mbappé received the most unwanted attention and blew up at his opposition and the referee several times.

Ousmane Dembélé, so electric with his hat trick against Norway, was smothered and effectively contained by Paraguay, while Michael Olise, so often a provider, could barely break free.

The South Americans created few opportunities, making some half breaks, but never any dangerous penetration into the final third, while France was forced into long-range speculative shots, with Adrien Rabiot a key culprit.

There were better signs of life in the second half. Goalkeeper Mike Maignan's excellent long ball for Mbappé almost saw the captain score but was shut down by an impressive last gasp effort from Jose Caceres.

And with a goal in open play looking impossible, VAR put France out of its misery.

Starved of a goal, sections of the crowd started chanting "penalty, penalty" without even seeing a replay, and there were massive cheers when it was awarded for Diego Gomez's trip on Désiré Doué.

After some needless delays from the Paraguayan defence, Mbappé stepped up, ran, stopped, then restarted and slotted the decisive penalty.

He came ever so close to closing it out in more impressive style in stoppage time, forcing a brilliant double save from Orlando Gill.

This was the most France has been pushed at this tournament, and yet never really looked in any danger of losing it.

It might be helpful to other teams, namely its next opposition Morocco, to see a France World Cup win is not a fait accompli, although to win when strangled in this manner is still an impressive feat.

After their round of 32 win against Sweden, the players acknowledged the French fans with applause but nothing raucous.

This time, as the celebratory anthem Freed from Desire blared through the speakers, they were jumping, waving their arms in the air, dancing and singing in synchronisation with the supporters.

A cursory look might suggest this was another routine win for a team steamrolling its way towards the title.

But it was a dogged test, and France will enjoy coming away from it largely unscathed.

Stay across the latest news, analysis and features from the 2026 FIFA World Cup here.

Marking Independence Day

To have an excited, engaged, capacity crowd in a largely dull game in oppressive heat says a lot about how much Americans have embraced the World Cup games they have hosted.

Large numbers of travelling supporters have attended all games, but they have been complemented by a very strong showing of locals who have wanted to experience this mega event, despite not being a "soccer" nation.

Leading up to this game, Philadelphia Independence National Historical Park, the site of the signing of the Declaration of Independence, was full of Americans who had travelled from across the country for the 250th celebrations.

But there were also many excited that the World Cup had provided an opportunity for the country to show a different side of itself.

"I think the World Cup is exactly what we needed right now," Donovan, from Alabama, said.

"There's a lot of political unrest in America, but having so many people from different countries, going to be so excited about everything that's happening in America.

"It's just reminding us about our foundation and why we built this country in the first place. And it's been a great wake-up call and a great reminder of why we're so special."

Julian is a Colombian American from San Francisco who has been travelling around the US for the 250th anniversary events and wanted to soak up the environment.

"I think it's surreal … kind of an out-of-body experience to just put yourself in that time," he said.

"And our history is not perfect, but it's our history nonetheless. And I think history we can respect, appreciate, and learn from. And I think that's the excitement that I get from just being in the atmosphere."

title: Australians cagey about champ Perry ahead of T20 World Cup final
text:
Ellyse Perry is set to grace another World Cup final as Australia's cricketers attempt to regain their familiar position as the best in the business in the women's T20 final at Lord's.

The showdown with hosts England in front of a 30,000 packed house at the home of cricket has the potential to be one of the great days in women's cricket, and there has been speculation over whether the injured 35-year-old Perry, who has played in all 10 editions of the T20 World Cup, will feature again.

Australia coach Shelley Nitschke hinted Australia could play the six-time winner on Monday morning at 12:20am AEST, even if she is not fully fit, after the quadricep "awareness" scare that forced Perry to retire hurt after facing just seven deliveries against the West Indies in Thursday's semifinal.

Ellyse Perry (left) and Sophie Molineux (right) speak with coach Shelley Nitschke at training. (Getty Images: Matthew Lewis)

Though Perry seemed to emerge from a second day of training at Lord's unscathed, captain Sophie Molineux was giving nothing away to old rivals England, as she seemed to suggest the allrounder might be restricted to just a batting role.

"Ellyse trained really well, had a good bat, had a good bowl, she will field as well," Molineux said.

"We will assess tomorrow, see how she pulls up before we make any decisions.

"It would be great if she can bowl for us, but at the same time, with our bowling depth, we feel like we have got cover there.

Loading Instagram content

"Her T20 World Cup so far with the bat, she has been incredible and has won us a couple of games. Even if she is not bowling, it will be a massive positive for us."

Something has to give in the clash of the tournament's two unbeaten sides. Australia, defeated in the semifinals of the last T20 and ODI World Cups, have looked particularly imperious in winning all six matches so far.

Back in a final for the first time since 2023, when they won the T20 title in South Africa, Molineux's side is itching to return to the winners' circle again after a rare three-year drought and a rebuilding of the team.

Australia starts as favourites again after beating England in a warm-up match and while protecting an extraordinary record of having won all six white-ball World Cup finals against England, while also claiming a 16-0 whitewash win in the last Ashes series 18 months ago.

Molineux said the team would see the tournament as a huge success regardless of the result.

"The girls have played with freedom, they have been brave. We have played a really amazing team game of cricket. We can take so much away from this tournament," she said.

"In terms of legacy, if we get over the line tomorrow, that will only add to that."

For Molineux personally, after taking the reins from retired Alyssa Healy, it would be a huge and emotional personal triumph.

Can't get enough cricket from Grandstand? Tune in for the latest cricket news, interviews and analysis from the ABC team in the Grandstand Cricket Podcast.

As a kid, she had always wanted to watch a Test match at Lord's with her dad, but she cannot help laughing that leading Australia out onto the field as captain in a World Cup final with her father watching from the stands eclipses that.

"It is really special," the 28-year-old skipper said.

"I grew up watching Test cricket with my dad, and I said to him 15 years ago, I wanted to watch a Test match at Lord's. Who would have thought we would be running out and playing a World Cup final here in front of a packed crowd?

"For us, Lord's is the home of cricket, and it is going to be a really special occasion."

England skipper Nat Sciver-Brunt is adamant there is no scarring in the England camp after so many defeats by the old enemy.

"Our team has come a long way since that Ashes tour, and there's not really been any talk about the Ashes or proving people wrong from that," Sciver-Brunt said.

"We're under no illusion that it's going to be a really tough game. Against Australia, finals like that don't come around that often, so we'll be relishing the challenge."

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.

AAP

title: Student balloons capture Earth images from 35 kilometres above Cobar
text:
A group of high school students from Newcastle have successfully launched two self-made high-altitude weather balloons from outback NSW.

The year 10 students, who are part of a STEM teaching unit at the Hunter School of Performing Arts, built two balloons to gather data from the Earth's atmosphere.

Twenty-one engineering students took part in the launch at a high school in Cobar on Wednesday.

"The reason we came all the way out to Cobar is we want to prevent [the balloons] from landing in the ocean or trees," said student Hart van de Wijgaart.

"It made it easier to retrieve."

Loading...

The balloons were made to travel 35 kilometres above the Earth, before bursting and gently returning to the ground with a parachute.

One of the balloon strings broke early and landed 40km north of Tottenham.

However, the second balloon made it to an altitude of 35km and landed 250km away from the original launch spot, at a citrus farm in Narromine.

Five-year-old Ezra Roberts, who lives on the orange farm with his parents, helped retrieve the balloon on Friday.

"I was riding my bike over to my dad and we found the box," Ezra said.

Five-year-old Ezra Roberts was part of the recovery team for the weather balloon. (ABC Western Plains: Lily Plass)

Passion project

Each balloon weighed 3 kilograms and was made from degradable latex.

It was attached to a box that carried a tracker, as well as equipment that collected data such as temperature, speed, location, humidity and pressure.

Some students also added personal trinkets.

"If we look at the website, we can see the altitude, longitude and where to find it on the map," Hart said.

The students added a few keepsakes to go on the journey into the upper atmosphere. (ABC Western Plains: Lily Plass)

The students raised $15,000 to financially cover the trip and materials.

Charlie Buchanan was part of the media team for the project.

"I thought the idea of us working as a class to create this one big project sounded awesome," she said.

"The fact we get to design and create this whole thing sounded really cool."

The weather conditions pushed the balloon further east than expected. (Supplied: Hunter School of Performing Arts)

Second time's a charm

It is not the first time the students have sent a weather balloon into the atmosphere.

The first balloon was launched in Nyngan in 2024 with then-school captain Nolan Sobel-Read, and travelled to an altitude of 28 kilometres.

According to the World Air Sports Federation, space officially begins about 100 kilometres above sea level at the Karman line, although there have been studies published that suggest space may start at 80 kilometres altitude.

The Newcastle students travelled nearly 700 kilometres to Cobar for the launch. (Supplied: Hunter School of Performing Arts )

Teacher Ben Moore said he had enjoyed the project so much he decided to repeat it with a bigger group.

"We were just really thrilled by the whole experience. We just had to bring it back and bigger," he said.

"It brings me a lot of pride and joy to see these guys put in all the hard work and see where the adventure takes us."

title: 'Never trust a label': Mystery violin willed to youth orchestra
text:
When not-for-profit organisation Music for Canberra received an email from a family it had never met, it was intrigued to find that they had been gifted an old, and very special, instrument.

It quickly became clear it hadn't just inherited a violin; it had inherited a mystery.

The instrument is evidently old, but how old is a subject of much discussion. Was it a $5,000 hidden gem or a $50,000 Spanish masterpiece?

The only paperwork it came with is a label just visible through the f-hole in the body of the instrument. It reads Matriti per Granadensem Josephum Contreras Anno 1752.

The label just visible through the f-hole in the body of the instrument reads Contreras Anno 1752. (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

'Never trust a label'

Michael Hargreaves, a luthier in Canberra who is lending his skills to restoring the violin and its bows, believes labels are notoriously unreliable.

"So, you'd never trust a label," Mr Hargreaves said.

The label could be a reference to Josephus (José) Contreras, a Granada-born luthier who studied in Italy and worked in Spain.

He worked in Madrid from about 1740 and was strongly influenced by the renowned Antonio Stradivari, whose instruments were becoming recognised in the Spanish court at that time.

The auction house Tarisio describes him as the greatest Spanish violin maker, known as Il Grenadino.

"He was known to have been a very good emulator of Italian instruments to the point where, over time, a number of his instruments have actually had his label taken out and a fake Italian label put in," Mr Hargreaves said.

Michael Hargreaves says he is hopeful the violin is authentic, but only forensic analysis can confirm its provenance. (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

And while Mr Hargreaves reiterates you should never trust a label, he is also not discounting it entirely.

"The fact that it's his label in there is kind of assuring because it hasn't been ripped off or emulated," he said.

He admits it gives him a bit of hope that the violin may be a Contreras, but it would require historians and forensic analysis to figure that out.

'It's not an amateur'

Tracing the violin's provenance has become part of the excitement.

It's the body of the instrument itself that has caught Mr Hargreaves's attention the most.

"My first impression was that it was done by an amateur," he said.

Cross-referencing wood grain patterns against regional databases could provide clues to where the wood of the violin came from. (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

"But when you look at it more and more, you see a lot of fine detail which says that no, it's not an amateur, it's actually somebody who really knows what they're doing."

He said the proportions and carving style echoed a particular lineage of European instrument-making, one that ran through the Amati family, whose most famous member had trained a young Antonio Stradivari.

"So, we're talking about something that's late 1600s, early 1700s, as far as the style goes," Mr Hargreaves said.

He said even if it was not backed up, it was still a special instrument because it was so unique.

"If there was provenance and if there was evidence that it was actually from Contreras in Spain in 1752, then that would just be a bit of a shot in the arm," he said.

Michael Hargreaves repairs, restores and builds instruments at his store Things with Strings. (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

Confirming the Contreras label

Confirming the violin's true age and maker isn't a matter of opinion. It requires forensic analysis using high-resolution photographs and technology well beyond the tools of the trade in his workshop.

"There's dendrochronology, which maps the growth rings in the wood so that they can determine which forest it came from and what year the tree was cut," Mr Hargreaves said.

Dendrochronology is a technique that has existed for a few decades and involves cross-referencing wood grain patterns against regional databases to give clues to where the wood came from.

"You can take a photograph of a section of the grain of the wood, and they can look at it, and they can put it across their database of maps, of growth rings for different regions, different forests, different eras," Mr Hargreaves said.

But the mystery of the violin may remain unless Music for Canberra decides the cost and effort are worth pursuing further.

Fixing things with strings

The restoration of the instrument ran from scroll to end pin, and included the violin's French-made Grandini bow and another crafted in what is now Czechia.

Grandini bows were made in Mirecourt, France, in the early- to mid-20th century and this one could be worth between $2,500 and $6,250.

"There were some cracks that had reopened. The bows were in terrible condition," Mr Hargreaves said.

Even the horsehair on the bow has been sourced from a very specific part of the world. (ABC News: Lish Fejer)

"They had considerably warped over the years and needed to be heated up and straightened and then re-cambered and then have all of their silver work and their leather work redone."

Even the horsehair on the bow has been sourced from a very specific part of the world.

"Siberia and Mongolia, I believe, are the main areas for sourcing horsehair for bows because they are winter horses and the hair grows thicker," Mr Hargreaves said.

He said he would write notes of what he had done to the violin, a little like the service history of a car, so luthiers centuries down the track would know what had been done to it.

"Instruments need work. They will last forever and they always need servicing. I'm just the next person to work on it over its life span," he said.

The trail of clues a previous luthier left for him were from Lloyd Adams, who left Australia in 1957 and died in 1974.

"He wrote a little tiny note on the sound post inside the body," Mr Hargreaves said.

But Mr Hargreaves admits he doesn't like writing inside instruments.

"If everybody did that, it'd just be full of graffiti."

A donation for young musicians

Mystery surrounds not just the instrument, but the generous donor as well.

Music For Canberra discovered it had been gifted the violin in a will when it received an email last November from the family of Thomas (Mitch) Powell from Broken Hill.

Mr Powell had passed away at the Harold Williams Home in Broken Hill in October 2025 at the age of 90.

Music for Canberra artistic director Rebecca Rivera believes Mr Powell had some interaction with the Canberra Youth Orchestra back in the 90s.

Rebecca Rivera says the violin is beautiful, regardless of its mysterious origins. (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

Canberra Youth Orchestra is one of the artistic programs under the Music for Canberra umbrella.

"But it left such a positive impression on him that he decided to leave his very special, high-quality violin to Canberra Youth Orchestra in his will," Dr Rivera said.

"It's a beautiful instrument. There was a lot of mystery surrounding it. Its provenance, we don't completely understand."

While the provenance is still unknown, the violin is almost ready to take centre stage.

Brad Tham, a 25-year-old violinist, says the instrument makes a very bright sound. (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

Brad Tham, a 25-year-old violinist who has been playing since he was eight years old, said it was a visually beautiful instrument.

"This one is a very bright sounding violin, and it's also very responsive. It handles very fast notes very easily," Mr Tham said.

Canberra Youth Orchestra plans to make it available as a scholarship violin, potentially for its concertmaster or a featured soloist in time for the organisation's 60th anniversary next year.

Whether it's ever conclusively linked to José Contreras or simply remains a very good instrument with a very good story, the mystery will always play second fiddle to the music for generations of young musicians to come.

title: Locals worried remote Gove Peninsular town won't survive housing crisis
text:
A senior Northern Territory government official has criticised a government-funded remote housing provider for "not doing enough" to support small business owners in a local housing crisis.

Rio Tinto is due to shut its bauxite mine near the town of Nhulunbuy, in north-east Arnhem Land, in 2029.

Rio Tinto accused of abandoning Nhulunbuy ahead of exit Nhulunbuy is preparing for its biggest employer to leave town, and locals are accusing the mining giant of squeezing out small businesses on its way out.

The operation has held up the local economy for decades through well-paid mining jobs and the flow of royalties to the region's traditional owners.

In the time leading up to the cessation of bauxite production, known locally as 'the transition', many long-term residents have called out a perceived lack of long-term planning for Nhulunbuy's future.

Residents who spoke to the ABC all named a critical shortage of housing as a major issue facing the town.

Nhulunbuy economic fund accused of forgetting its purpose

Following the closure of an alumina refinery in 2014, the NT government and Rio Tinto established an economic development agency called Developing East Arnhem Land (DEAL), providing it with 250 local houses for businesses to accommodate staff.

Local residents say Nhulunbuy needs more housing. (ABC News: Dane Hirst)

DEAL manages and rents out that housing to government agencies, Aboriginal corporations and local employers via a waiting list.

Only 17 per cent of Nhulunbuy's housing is managed by DEAL, with the remainder owned privately, by the NT government or Rio Tinto.

Blue Douglas runs a handful of small businesses in Nhulunbuy with his family, but said he was planning on selling up and leaving town due to the economic uncertainty and lack of available housing.

Mr Douglas said he had attended meetings where DEAL had committed to supporting local businesses as part of their remit.

Local business owner Blue Douglas plans on packing up and leaving town amid the uncertainty. (ABC News: Dane Hirst)

"We were informed way back when [DEAL] started that they were here to assist and develop business. These days their focus seems to be on tourism," he said.

" I understand they do other things in the background, but for the average small business like me they're [of] very, very little assistance. "

According to DEAL's website, their housing portfolio "is currently at full capacity".

Mr Douglas said it was business owners who were missing out.

Only part of Nhulunbuy's housing is managed by DEAL, and locals say they are missing out. (ABC News: Dane Hirst)

"We can't get accommodation, which means we can't hire and retain staff, which means we can't grow our business," he said.

Earlier this year, DEAL launched a major tourism campaign under its Visit East Arnhem brand, with a series of advertisements showcasing the region's natural environment, Yolŋu culture and outdoor lifestyle.

The tourism campaign, and other initiatives such as small business grants, are partly funded by income generated through DEAL's housing management.

Visit East Arnhem's tourism campaign launched earlier this year. (Supplied: Visit East Arnhem)

Louise McCormick, a senior public servant within the NT government and chair of the Gove Peninsula Futures Reference Group (GPFRG), of which DEAL is a member, said residents like Mr Douglas had a point.

"I specifically have said to [DEAL] I'd like more focus on small business, because that's the original remit for DEAL," she said.

" I think they have probably broadened their remit a little too wide, and need to focus in on what transition means for our businesses in that town. "

A spokesperson for DEAL rejected the criticism.

Louise McCormick chairs the Gove Peninsula Futures Reference Group. (ABC News: Pete Garnish)

"The DEAL remit has never changed," they said.

"The focus remains on small businesses.

"DEAL's remit has always been industry development and industry attraction inter alia (among other things) economic development, including small business."

The not-for-profit declined to provide a breakdown of its housing allocations, but said its "primary purpose" was to support business.

It also declined to say if Rio Tinto would hand over any more housing stock before 2029.

Rio Tinto's local bauxite mine will close in 2029, with residents anxious about how the town will transition into the future. (ABC News: Dane Hirst)

Mc McCormick said future planning for Nhulunbuy's housing market would be the subject of an investigation by the GPFRG, which is due to front the Nhulunbuy community at a public meeting this month.

Traditional owners attempt to address housing shortage

In a cul-de-sac in Nhulunbuy's west, construction is nearing completion on an $8 million housing development.

The chief executive of its developer, the Rirratjingu Aboriginal Corporation, has been frank about the town's housing availability.

"It's very expensive [and] in terms of availability, it's zero," Paul Martin said.

Rirratjingu's chair Wanyubi Marika said the investment had been made with royalties accrued from a deal between the Rirratjingu, the traditional owners of the Nhulunbuy township, the Gumatj, the traditional owners for the Rio Tinto mine site area, and Rio Tinto.

Wanyubi Marika is the chair of Rirratjingu Aboriginal Corporation. (ABC News: Dane Hirst)

"This is our family money, putting it into reality for survival of the future of Gove," Mr Marika said.

Nhulunbuy's township lease has become a critical part of transition planning, with new housing only able to be developed by the Rirratjingu on land relinquished by Rio Tinto.

Rio Tinto's leasehold over Nhulunbuy does not expire until 2053, and Mr Marika said he was keen to see further detail on the timeline of a hand-back as part of ongoing talks with the mining giant and the Northern Land Council.

"We've never seen [what] your plans are, so put it in the open where the public can see," he said.

The NT government has already signed leases for its local health workers at Rirratjingu's 22-bedroom development, and Ms McCormick said the NT government would relinquish some of its leases on DEAL's housing to relieve rental demand.

A number of houses currently under construction in Nhulunbuy have been pre-leased to the NT government. (ABC News: Dane Hirst)

She said it was realistic to expect that a decision would be made as to when and how Rio Tinto would stage the hand-over of Nhulunbuy's lease, by the end of next year.

"That gives us an 18-month window to have a much greater focus then on economic attraction into the region," she said.

title: Fran Lebowitz — witty, withering and unfiltered
text:
Fran Lebowitz is smoking one cigarette after another as she tells me why she never intends to visit Australia again.

At 75, the celebrated American critic, writer and documentary star is chatting with me in a designated outdoor smoking zone before we're due on stage together, where I'll interview her in front of a live audience.

We're mere minutes away from showtime, but Lebowitz is already lighting another cigarette before finishing her first one.

Fran Lebowitz started smoking when she was 12. (Compass: Danielle Bonica)

Because of Lebowitz's tobacco dependency ("an addiction", she concedes while shrugging), she says the crushingly long flights from her beloved New York City to Australia are akin to torture.

When her manager and I remind her she said something similar when I last interviewed her in Australia, several years back, Lebowitz arches an eyebrow and shrugs, as if to say: So?

When our conversation digresses to other drugs, we discuss how people are increasingly micro-dosing MDMA and psilocybin to manage conditions like PTSD.

Lebowitz blinks in confusion.

"Excuse me, why are they doing this again?" she asks.

"They're managing mental distress," I say.

Old friends Fran Lebowitz and Martin Scorsese share an intimate laugh. (Kevin Mazur/VF12: Getty Images)

Lebowitz lets out a trademark withering laugh — a laugh made famous by two Martin Scorsese documentaries on her, a myriad late night talk show interviews and, now, social media memes.

" Mental distress?" she says. "Who isn't mentally distressed? That's just being alive in 2026. "

Fran Lebowitz made a name for herself in the 1970s thanks to her sharp wit and her writing. (Yvonne Hemsey: Getty Images)

Fran Lebowitz is a singular figure in American culture. She is a critic, but does not have a regular column. She is an author, but hasn't published a book since 1994. She is funny, but not a stand-up comedian.

Yet people across the English-speaking world, and plenty of non-English speaking ones too, flock to Lebowitz's live speaking tours to hear her offer quips, bon mots and scathing take-downs of New York's peculiarities, poor etiquette and young and old people alike.

No-one emerges unscathed.

However, given the times, she is particularly appalled about the state of US politics.

"No-one could have imagined Donald Trump," she says on stage. She also describes the "moral squalor" of institutions designed to keep presidential powers in check, like the US Supreme Court.

"It never occurred to the founders of the country that the people who would get to the Supreme Court would need ethics rules," she says.

" The Supreme Court now is so disgraceful that it shouldn't even be allowed to be called the Supreme Court. "

Lebowitz famously also shuns modern technology, having refused to engage with technology since the landline phone and colour TV.

Needless to say, she doesn't own a smartphone. It's not that she hasn't kept up, she clarifies. It's just that she's just not very interested in it.

"When they invented the kind of computer you have in your house, a friend of mine who is a screenwriter said, 'You have to come and see this,'" Lebowitz recalls. "So I went and I said, 'This is just a very fast kind of typewriter.'" She scoffs at the memory now.

" I don't need anything this fast. I write so slowly, I could write with my own blood without hurting myself. "

Fran Lebowitz has become one of America's most distinctive voices. (Compass: Danielle Bonica)

On stage, I ask Lebowitz to explain to us how she even arrived at Melbourne's Hamer Hall for this event without a smartphone. After all, how does one travel internationally in 2026 without digital maps, email or rideshare apps? Without missing a beat, Lebowitz says: "I have an agent."

She is not exactly enamoured with other people's use of smartphones either. "On the subway — but also at the airport, and also everywhere — everyone is looking at their phones," she observes. "I'm pretty sure if I did [have one], I would never think to look at it while I was trying to cross Seventh Avenue."

Fran Lebowitz and Andy Warhol were often seen together in the 1970s. (Getty Images: Richard E. Aaron/Redferns)

One might suspect young people would consider Lebowitz out of touch. After all, this 75-year-old American critic rose through the ranks of legacy print media, was hired by the late Andy Warhol as a writer for Interview magazine and does not access the internet. How many millennials or Gen Zs would recognise a 70-something public figure like Fran Lebowitz anyway?

Plenty, it turns out. When Pretend It's a City — a seven-episode 2021 Netflix documentary by Martin Scorsese on Lebowitz — was released, it coincided with global Covid lockdowns. With a literal captive audience, Lebowitz was introduced to entirely new generations without even needing to catch an aeroplane.

Benjamin Law has been lucky enough to interview Fran Lebowitz several times. (Compass: Danielle Bonica)

Locked-down, grumpy and misanthropic, audiences tuned in to watch a similarly cantankerous person — albeit an acerbically-funny Jewish New York lesbian in her 70s — cheerily point out everything that was wrong with the world. No matter what their generation, gender or backgrounds, people saw themselves reflected in Lebowitz, albeit with a wink back, a trademark laugh and a cloud of cigarette smoke.

Fran Lebowitz is backstage during what may be her final visit to Australia. (Compass: Danielle Bonica)

Fran Lebowitz on:

NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani

"A lot of my friends who are my contemporaries say, 'Don't you think he's too young?' He's 34. I have to explain to my friends: 'We are now so old that you can be much-much-much younger than us and still not be young.' So he's not too young to be the mayor, not at all. My concern about him was though is he's a little delicate looking. He looks like a piano student."

Death

"I do think about it sometimes. I don't want to die, but sometimes when I hear certain things in the news I think, 'Well, don't worry, you'll be dead.' But I have to tell you, I have terrible seasickness. If you ever are seasick, here's what you notice: There are people dying of awful, painful diseases and they want to live. If you're seasick? You want to die."

Polyamory

"It doesn't appeal to me. I know it's a thing; I know people think it's new. It's not new. Maybe this doesn't sound nice, but I don't care what other people do. I have almost no interest in it. Do whatever you want, except make noise. Four lovers? Sixteen? I don't care, but do it quietly. I live in an apartment building, so I don't really care what's going on next door upstairs. Just sshh."

Trad wives

"This is all part of an unbelievable regression. These same people point out how horrible it is for women in very traditional Muslim countries or for observant, crazy Orthodox Jews, but it's exactly like this. Regular Jews like me call those Jews 'crazy Jews'. Gentiles are always upset when you do that, but it's all the same stuff. What all these extreme versions of religions have in common is oppressing women. These women say they like it, but they don't get around that much."

Legacy

"People often ask me, 'What do you want your legacy to be?' I don't care. What do you want people to think about you after you die? I don't care now. Some people really care about it, especially rich people, with their names over all these buildings. I always think every time they do that, 'Well, that's nice, but you're going to die anyway.'

Watch Fran Lebowitz with Benjamin Law on Compass tonight at 6:30pm on ABC TV or stream now on iview.

title: Hey now, you’re an All-Star: Australian rookie Bazzana makes history in US baseball league
text:
Advertisement

SportBaseball Hey now, you’re an All-Star: Australian rookie Bazzana makes history in US baseball league Sebastian Tan July 5, 2026 — 1:49pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

The remarkable rise and rise of Australia’s Travis Bazzana has continued, with the 23-year-old earning a maiden call-up for Major League Baseball’s All-Star game. Bazzana made history when he joined the Cleveland Guardians as the No.1 pick in the 2024 MLB draft, marking the first time an Australian had ever been drafted in the first round. Australian Travis Bazzana has made a sudden impact in baseball’s Major League. Getty Images The NSW product made his MLB debut on April 28 this year and has already produced some eye-catching form, resulting in a call-up for the Mid-Summer Classic in Philadelphia on July 14 (July 15 AEST). Bazzana becomes just the fourth Australian to receive an All-Star nod, following in the footsteps of Dave Nilsson (1999), Grant Balfour (2013) and Liam Hendriks (2019, 2021, 2022).

Advertisement

The infielder has been selected in the American league’s squad as a player-elected reserve. “It puts perspective on things,” Bazzana said. View post on X “I do set high expectations for myself. I’m going to continue to work. I think there’s a lot left that I can bring to the team on the field moving forward. “Obviously, a good perspective to give to myself when I sometimes don’t give myself grace.

Advertisement

“Hopefully, we continue to have more All-Stars and more players that believe they can go and do really amazing things in MLB.” Bazzana and teammate Parker Messick have notably been picked while rookies, a feat that has only been achieved at the Guardians by three previous players in franchise history. Bazzana covering second base for the Guardians. Getty Images It is just the fifth occasion that one MLB club has supplied multiple rookies in an All-Star game, with the most recent instance being the New York Yankees in 2014. Bazzana has been instrumental in helping the Guardians push for a third consecutive post-season berth, becoming a regular at the top of Cleveland’s line-up.

Advertisement

Related Article Australia’s richest athletes

Business of sport Australian sport’s top earners: Counting down from 25 to one Across 58 games, the Australian has whacked seven home runs, 12 doubles and 27 runs batted in (RBIs), with a batting average of .250 and an OBP (on-base percentage) of .341. Those numbers helped Bazzana receive All-Star selection through player balloting, a system where the competition’s players, managers and coaches vote to select the remaining pitchers and back-up players for the All-Star clash.
